text: This video presents a robotic radial hepatectomy, a resection segment IV-B, seven years after open right hemohepatectomy. The patient is a seventy-five year old male with a history of child acerosis. In two thousand fourteen he underwent an open total mesorectal excision with ileostomy for rectal cancer. There were five synchronous colorectal liver metastases for which he received four cycles of neoadjuvant chemotherapy Folfox. followed by open right hemipotectomy with partial resection of segment IV-A. Postoperatively, eight cycles of adjuvant chemotherapy full flux were administered. He underwent idiostomy closure a few months later.

At routine oncologic follow-up at the beginning of the year, he was diagnosed with a solitary colorectal liver metastasis in segment IV-B. On the PET and MRI scan, you may see the liver metastases in segment IVb. The maximal diameter of the tumor was measured at thirty-seven millimeters. The patient was planned for an upfront liver resection. A robotic approach was chosen as compared to laparoscopy, robotic surgery allows to operate more accurately and intuitively thanks to three-D visualization and embryo risk technology. According to the Southampton Laparoscopic Liver Resection Difficulty Score, which is based on the five preoperative factors as shown on the slide, intraoperative risk was calculated to be fifty four point seven percent, which classified the surgery as extremely high risk.

The patient is positioned supine with a 12-degree anti-Trendelenburg tilt. Visible scars include a midline intra-umbilical incision, a right subcostal scar, and a previous ileostomy scar. A large incisional hernia is observed at the lateral aspect of the subcostal scar. Four 8-mm robotic ports are placed, including a supra-umbilical camera port and three additional ports. An air-sealed 12-mm assistant port is positioned in the left iliac region.

The surgeon performs extensive adhesiolysis using monopolar scissors and a bipolar sealing device to free the liver from the diaphragm. Segment IVb is particularly challenging to free due to its location at the previous transection plane. The liver hilum is cleared of adhesions to facilitate the Pringle maneuver, executed using the Hwang-Loop technique with a Foley catheter around the hepatoduodenal ligament. Intraoperative ultrasound is used to accurately identify the tumor.

The surgeon marks the transection line on the liver surface using monopolar scissors. After demarcating the lesion, a traction suture is placed with a resorbable multifilament suture and a hemo-lock. The Pringle maneuver is performed by applying traction on the Foley catheter, and a bulldog clamp is used to ensure effective inflow control.

The surgeon progressively resects the liver lesion using a bipolar sealing device, which seals and cuts tissue while performing caliclamping to identify portal pedicles. Vessels and bile ducts are clipped with hemo-locks and transected using the bipolar device. Parenchymal transection continues until the tumor is fully resected. Gauzes are placed on the transected liver parenchyma, and gentle pressure along with a sprinkle of water is applied to check for bleeding and bile leaks.

The specimen is placed in an amber catch bag within the abdomen. The gauze is removed, revealing a bile leak on the transected liver surface. The surgeon inspects the site and controls the bile leak using a barred monofilament suture. The Pringle maneuver is released by removing the bulldog clamp and loosening the Foley catheter. Hemostasis is checked, and Surgiflo hemostatic matrix is applied to areas with residual bleeding. The Foley catheter is then removed.

The surgeon applies a fibrillar absorbable hemostat to ensure hemostasis. Attention is then directed to repairing the incisional hernia, opting for primary closure using a barbed monofilament resorbable suture without mesh placement. A silicone drain is left in place due to a perioperative bile leak. The specimen is exteriorized by enlarging the incision.

Operative time counted as a hundred and seventy minutes. Blood loss was thirty cc and the Pringle maneuver was performed for eighteen minutes. Pathology confirmed an R-zero resection of a codorectal liver metastasis. The postoperative course was uneventful and the patient was discharged on day two postoperatively.
